text.title: Resource Planning: Roster, Utilisation, Skills, Ramp
text.summary: Rules for building a YAML roster in git, setting utilisation targets, maintaining the skill matrix, and accounting for ramp time and vacation.

rule.statement: Store allocations in git as resources/roster.yaml and resources/allocations.yaml, not in spreadsheets. Git diffs explain why allocations changed; spreadsheets erase history.
rule.rationale: Allocation decisions are context-dependent; understanding why a decision was made requires the diff, not just the current state.

rule.statement: Plan to 70% utilisation, not 100%. Reserve 30% per person per week for meetings, unplanned support, learning, and context switching. Flag any allocation exceeding 85% as RED immediately.
rule.rationale: 100% allocation means any unplanned event — a sick day, an urgent bug, a stakeholder request — causes a miss. 70% is the empirically sustainable peak for knowledge workers.

rule.statement: Rate cards live in 1Password or equivalent secrets store, referenced by ID only in the roster. Never commit monetary rates to the repo.
rule.rationale: Rate cards are commercially sensitive and, in some jurisdictions, legally protected compensation data. Committing them to git creates uncontrolled disclosure risk.

rule.statement: Skill matrix entries are dated, self-asserted, and peer-endorsed. Refresh quarterly. Agents must read skills only from the roster YAML — never infer skills from job titles.
rule.rationale: Job titles are unreliable skill proxies. An agent that assumes "Senior Engineer" knows Kubernetes and assigns them a Kubernetes task without checking the roster creates a skill gap that surfaces at the worst time.

rule.statement: Cross-train pairs for any single point of failure: one person who is the only team member knowing a critical system is a staffing risk. Escalate to the resource manager within one sprint of identifying it.
rule.rationale: SPOF skills create delivery risk that materialises on the one week the critical person is unavailable. Cross-training is the only durable mitigation.

rule.statement: Account for ramp time explicitly: a new joiner is 30–50% productive for 4–8 weeks. Under-counting ramp time is a systematic cause of the first milestone slip.
rule.rationale: Ramp time is predictable and consistent across roles, yet it is omitted from resource plans in the majority of projects. Treating a new hire as full capacity from day one is a planning error.

rule.statement: Lock in PTO and public holidays before committing to milestone dates. Vacation calendars are inputs to the schedule, not afterthoughts.
rule.rationale: Holiday and vacation conflicts discovered after milestone commitments require scope reduction or timeline extension. Discovery before commitment costs a calendar API call.
